blocks.text:
Michelle Roberts
Chief Financial Officer
Dyer and Sons
Re: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment, {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}
Dear Ms. Roberts:

This letter sets out the terms on which this firm will conduct a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment of the subject property, and asks you to countersign it so the work can be put in the schedule. We expect the assessment to run about four months from commencement to submission of the final report. No field work has been performed, and none will be until a countersigned copy of this letter is back in our hands.
Scope and Approach

The assessment will be performed in general conformance with ASTM E1527-13 and the All Appropriate Inquiries rule at 40 CFR Part 312. In practice that means four things, in order. Standard historical sources will be reviewed, including aerial photography, fire insurance maps, the topographic series, and such county records as exist on fill, grading, and shoreline work. Regulatory database records will be obtained for the property and for the search radii the standard prescribes. A site reconnaissance will be conducted covering the property, and the adjoining properties as they can be observed from the property and from public rights of way. Interviews will be conducted with persons reasonably likely to have knowledge of the property's past use.

The estuarine setting adds a step we do not skip. Tidal influence, fill history, and past shoreline work are exactly the things the standard sources record poorly, and on a parcel of this kind they are usually where the answer lives. The reconnaissance will therefore be scheduled against the tide, so that the water edge of the parcel is walked rather than described from forty feet away at high water.

The deliverable is a written report identifying recognized environmental conditions, controlled recognized environmental conditions, and historical recognized environmental conditions as those terms are defined in the standard, together with the data gaps encountered and our opinion of whether they are significant. Where the record does not support a conclusion, the report will say so in those words. That is a professional obligation rather than a hedge for its own sake, and I would rather write down what is not known than let a reader assume it was checked. A draft will be issued for your review, and one round of comment is included before the final is submitted.

The following are outside this scope: sampling of soil, sediment, groundwater, or building materials; any wetland delineation; and any Phase II work. If the assessment turns up a condition that warrants sampling, we will describe the condition, stop, and quote the additional work separately rather than proceed on our own authority.
Project Team

I will serve as Managing Principal and will read the report before it leaves this office, as I do with everything a lender or an agency will eventually see. John Sanchez, our Senior Scientist, will lead the records review and the reconnaissance. Mary Foster, Field Technician, will support the reconnaissance and keep the field record, including conditions, weather, and tide state on each visit. James Henderson, Environmental Scientist, will review the database findings and is the person to call if the scope has to open into sampling. All of us are available to Dyer and Sons through the course of the work; day to day, John Sanchez is the right first call.
Fee and Schedule

The work will be performed for a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2016-001.fee}}, covering the historical records review, the database review, the site reconnaissance, the interviews, the draft and final reports, and one round of comment on the draft. Work will commence on {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}}, subject to receipt of this letter countersigned and to site access. From that date we expect roughly four months to the final submission. The schedule assumes access to the property on reasonable notice and reasonable cooperation from the parties we ask to interview. Delay in one or the other is the most common reason a schedule of this kind slips, and I would rather say so now than explain it in September.
Fees and Payment

Invoices will be issued monthly against progress and are payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Out-of-pocket costs, including the database order and reproduction of the historical sources, are included in the fixed fee stated above; there is no separate expense line on this engagement. Work outside the scope described above will not be performed without your written authorization and a separately agreed fee. Amounts unpaid after sixty days may, at our option, suspend further work, and you will have that in writing from me before it happens rather than after.
Term and Termination

The engagement begins on the commencement date stated above and ends on delivery of the final report, unless extended in writing. Either party may terminate on ten days' written notice. On termination you will owe that portion of the fixed fee corresponding to work performed through the termination date, and we will deliver the work product in whatever condition it then exists, together with the field record. Obligations that by their nature survive termination, including confidentiality and payment for work performed, survive it.
Confidentiality

Information you provide, and the findings of the assessment, will be treated as confidential and will not be released to a third party without your written consent, except where disclosure is required by law or by an agency with jurisdiction. That exception is not boilerplate. A condition carrying a statutory reporting duty will be reported, and you will hear it from us before or at the time it goes to the agency, never afterward. Our field notes and working files remain the property of this firm. The report is yours, for the purpose for which it was prepared.
Limitation of Liability

A Phase I assessment is an inquiry into reasonably ascertainable information within the scope of the standard. It is not a certification that the property is free of contamination, and no such certification is offered or implied. Findings rest on conditions as observed on the dates of the reconnaissance and on information available when the report is issued; conditions change, and records surface late. To the fullest extent permitted by law, the aggregate liability of this firm to Dyer and Sons arising out of this engagement, whether in contract, tort, or otherwise, is limited to the amount of the fee actually paid for the work. Reliance on the report is limited to the client named above; extension of reliance to any other party requires our written agreement.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the laws of the state in which the subject property is situated, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions, and any action arising under it will be brought in the courts of that state. Before either of us files anything, both of us agree to sit down once, in the same room, with the people who actually did the work.

If the foregoing reflects your understanding, please sign both copies where indicated and return one to my attention. Once it is back we will confirm the reconnaissance dates with you and set them against the tide. Please call me directly with any question on the scope or the terms; I would rather answer it now than have it raised for the first time in the report.
Sincerely,
Memorandum
Objectives

The letter came back countersigned and the job opened on {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}}. This memo is the record of what we settled at the table this morning, written down now so that nobody has to reconstruct it in August out of three different recollections. The objective is a Phase I report this office can put its name on: historical records, database, reconnaissance, interviews, and a written opinion on recognized environmental conditions with the data gaps stated rather than smoothed over. Four months end to end. We are at the front of it. Nothing has been walked, nothing has been ordered, and the file was opened this week.

Two constraints shape the sequence. The aerial and map orders have the longest turnaround, so they go first even though the reconnaissance is the part everyone wants to talk about. And the parcel is tidal, which means the reconnaissance dates are set by the tide table and not by our calendar. Both of those are in the workstreams below.
Workstreams

items: Historical records review. John Sanchez has the standard sources: aerial photography, fire insurance maps, the topographic series, and whatever the county holds on fill, grading, and shoreline permits. He wants the aerial run ordered this week because the turnaround is the long pole and everything downstream waits on it.

Regulatory database review. James Henderson is placing the database order and intends to read the results rather than paste them. His request at the table, in his words: nobody quotes a listing to the client until he has looked at the distance and the direction himself.

Site reconnaissance. Two visits planned, both on a falling tide so the water edge of the parcel can actually be walked. I keep the field record: date, weather, tide state, who was present, what was observed, and what was looked for and not found.
Interviews. Michelle Roberts is arranging access and is our point of contact for the owner and occupant interviews. John Sanchez runs them and I take the notes.
Reporting. Draft to the client for one round of comment, then final. Nothing leaves this office unread by the Managing Principal, including the draft.

blocks.text: Ground Rules

Three things from the discussion are worth having in writing. First, what we observed and what we were told go in separate sentences, in the field record and in the report both; the distinction is easy to keep on the day and impossible to reconstruct later. Second, if a listing or a photograph suggests sampling, we describe the condition and stop. Sampling is not in this scope and James Henderson quotes it separately if it comes to that. Third, the photo log starts at the first visit, not at the second, and every frame gets a location and a direction while we are still standing there.
Next Steps

items: Aerial photography and map orders placed by end of week (John Sanchez).
Database order placed, confirmation logged to the file (James Henderson).
Access dates confirmed with Michelle Roberts and checked against the tide table before they are promised to anyone (Mary Foster).
Interview list drafted, owner and current occupant at a minimum (John Sanchez).
Field forms printed, site file opened, photo log started at the first visit (Mary Foster).

blocks.text: I will circulate a short summary after each field day rather than hold it all for the draft, on the theory that the odd detail is the one somebody needs six months from now and it is cheap to write down while it is fresh. Corrections to this memo come to me. I would rather fix the record this week than argue about it in the fall. Copy to the site file for Dyer and Sons.
Minutes of Working Session
Re: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment for {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}. Minutes recorded by John Sanchez and circulated for correction.
Attendees

items: John Sanchez, Senior Scientist, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners (chair)
Mary Foster, Field Technician, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners
James Henderson, Environmental Scientist, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners
Michelle Roberts, Chief Financial Officer, Dyer and Sons (by telephone)

blocks.text: Discussion

The historical sources are in hand and were reviewed at the table. The aerial series shows a change in the shoreline configuration between two of the earlier flights, consistent with placed fill along the northern margin of the parcel. It was noted that the interval between those two flights is long enough that the date of the work cannot be fixed from the photography alone, and that the fire insurance coverage does not extend to this part of the shore. The county permit file was searched and no shoreline permit was located for the interval in question. Absence of a permit record is not evidence that the work was unpermitted; it was agreed that the report will state what was searched and what was not found, in those terms, and will carry the interval as a data gap unless the interviews close it.

The database results were reviewed by James Henderson before the session and were summarized by him at it. Several listings fall within the standard search radii. He observed that two of them are upgradient only if the gradient is assumed to follow surface topography, which on a tidal parcel is an assumption and not a finding, and he declined to characterize them further until the reconnaissance settles the question. One listing appeared with coordinates that place it in open water, which he flagged as a plotting error in the source rather than a condition, and he will confirm that with the vendor rather than carry it into a table unremarked.

The first reconnaissance was completed and was described from the field record. The visit was made on a falling tide and the water edge was walked in full. Fill material was observed at the northern margin, exposed in a low bank, and was photographed and logged. Mary Foster noted that the exposure is tide-dependent and was not visible from the upland side at all, which is the reason the visit was scheduled the way it was. No staining, no drums, no aboveground or apparent underground storage tanks, and no distressed vegetation were observed on the property. She was careful at the table to separate what she saw from what an adjoining occupant told her about past use of the northern margin; the latter is in the notes as hearsay pending the formal interview.

Michelle Roberts asked when a conclusion could be expected on the fill. The answer given was that a second visit and the owner and occupant interviews come first, and that if the origin and character of the fill cannot be established from those, the report will say that it could not be established and will state what that means for the inquiry. She asked whether that would satisfy a lender. It was explained that a stated data gap with a reasoned opinion on its significance is what the standard contemplates, and that the alternative, a confident call the record does not support, is the one that gets overturned later. She accepted the position and asked that the draft flag the point plainly rather than bury it. The second visit and the interview schedule were discussed; access is being arranged through Dyer and Sons and no dates were fixed at the session.
Action Items

rows: Confirm the plotting error on the open-water listing with the database vendor; log the response to the file
Search the county file a second time under the prior owner name and under the parcel's earlier designation
Schedule the second reconnaissance on a falling tide; confirm the window against the tide table before it is promised
Draft interview questions for the owner and the adjoining occupant, focused on the fill interval
Arrange access and the interview introductions
Reconcile the hearsay in the field notes against the formal interview record; keep the two distinct

blocks.text: Corrections to these minutes should be sent to the chair within one week. Absent correction, they stand as the record of the session.
Progress Report
Re: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment for {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}. Prepared by Mary Foster, Field Technician, and reviewed before issue.
Status Summary

The assessment is at roughly the halfway mark of a four-month engagement and is on schedule. The historical records review is complete. The regulatory database review is complete except for one item awaiting a vendor correction. Both site reconnaissance visits have been made. The interviews are underway. Nothing has been drafted for report yet; that begins once the interview record is closed. One open question, the origin and date of fill at the northern margin of the parcel, is the item most likely to shape what the report can and cannot conclude, and it is discussed under Risks below.
Accomplishments This Period

The historical sources are reviewed and filed: the aerial series, the topographic sheets, the fire insurance coverage such as it is for this part of the shore, and the county permit and grading files. John Sanchez searched the county file twice, the second time under the prior owner name and the parcel's earlier designation, which turned up two grading records that the first search did not. Neither addresses the shoreline work.

Both reconnaissance visits are done. Each was made on a falling tide and the water edge was walked in full on both. The field record for each visit carries the date, weather, tide state, personnel present, and a photo log with location and direction for every frame. Observed at the northern margin: fill material exposed in a low bank, photographed on both visits, with the exposure visible only from the water side and only at lower stages of the tide. Not observed anywhere on the property, on either visit: staining, drums, aboveground or apparent underground storage tanks, distressed vegetation, or evidence of dumping. I record the second list because in this work what was looked for and not found is as much a part of the record as what was found, and a reader in five years cannot tell the difference between not present and not checked unless we say which it was.

James Henderson has closed out the database review. He confirmed with the vendor that one listing plotting in open water is an error in the source rather than a condition on the ground, and the vendor's written correction is in the file. He also resolved the gradient question raised earlier: the topographic assumption does not hold on a tidal parcel, and his characterization of the nearby listings now rests on what the reconnaissance showed rather than on an inference from contours. Two listings that were provisionally carried as upgradient are not.
The owner interview has been completed. The interview of the adjoining occupant is scheduled and not yet held.
Upcoming Work

items: Complete the adjoining occupant interview and close the interview record.
Reconcile the field notes against the formal interview record, keeping observation and hearsay distinct in both.
Prepare the draft report, including the findings, the data gaps, and our opinion on their significance.
Internal review of the draft by the Managing Principal before it is issued to Dyer and Sons.
Issue the draft for your review and one round of comment, then final.

blocks.text: Budget

The engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2016-001.fee}}, and effort to date is consistent with that figure at this point in the work. The database order and the historical source reproduction are inside the fee, as stated in the engagement letter, and there are no expense items to report. Nothing performed this period was outside the agreed scope, and no additional authorization is being requested. The second county search and the vendor correction were absorbed within the fee.
Risks

The fill at the northern margin remains unresolved. The aerial interval that brackets the work is long, the fire insurance coverage does not reach that part of the shore, and no shoreline permit was located in either county search. If the remaining interview does not establish the origin and approximate date of the fill, the report will carry the point as a data gap and state our opinion of its significance rather than resolve it by assertion. John Sanchez has been consistent on this and I will not soften it here: a confident call the record does not support is the one a reviewer takes apart later, and this office would rather write down the limit of what is known.

The schedule risk is access for the remaining interview, which is being arranged through Dyer and Sons. It is not currently causing delay. If the interview cannot be held within the next two weeks, the draft slips by roughly that interval, and you would hear about it from us before it happened rather than in the next report.
No other risks are identified at this time. Questions on any of the above can go to John Sanchez or to me.
